packs.name: kubectl
packs.description: Protects against destructive kubectl operations like delete namespace, drain, and mass deletion

structural_patterns.name: delete-all-massive
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl delete all --all removes EVERY supported resource in the current namespace. Extremely destructive!
structural_patterns.explanation:
This command deletes all supported Kubernetes resources (Deployments, Services, Pods, ConfigMaps, etc.) in the target namespace simultaneously. This will stop your entire application immediately and is almost never intended for live environments.

Verify and preview first:
  kubectl get all -n <namespace>
  kubectl delete all --all --dry-run=client

Target specific resources instead:
  kubectl delete deployment <name>

suggestions.description: Preview massive deletion without making changes
List all resources to review before deleting

structural_patterns.name: context-switch
structural_patterns.reason: Modifying Kubernetes contexts affects which cluster you are targeting. Ensure you are not affecting production accidentally.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Changing or deleting a context in your kubeconfig affects which cluster your future commands will target. Accidentally switching to a production cluster or deleting a necessary context can lead to downtime or unintended destructive operations.

Review current context:
  kubectl config current-context

List all available contexts:
  kubectl config get-contexts

suggestions.description: Verify your current active context
List all available contexts to find the right one

structural_patterns.name: kubectx-switch
structural_patterns.reason: Switching Kubernetes contexts via kubectx affects where future commands are executed.
structural_patterns.explanation:
kubectx is a tool for fast context switching. Changing the current context can lead to accidental operations in the wrong cluster.

Check current context:
  kubectx -c

suggestions.description: Show the current active context

structural_patterns.name: delete-namespace
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl delete namespace removes the entire namespace and ALL resources within it.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a namespace destroys EVERYTHING inside it:

- All deployments, pods, services
- All configmaps and secrets
- All persistent volume claims (data may be lost)
- All ingresses and network policies
- All RBAC resources scoped to the namespace

This is irreversible. Even if you recreate the namespace, all resources are gone.

Preview what would be deleted:
  kubectl get all -n <namespace>
  kubectl get pvc -n <namespace>

Safer approach:
  kubectl delete deployment <name> -n <namespace>  # Delete specific resources

suggestions.description: Preview what would be deleted without making changes
See all resources in the namespace before deleting
Allow graceful shutdown with 60-second grace period

structural_patterns.name: delete-all
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl delete --all removes ALL resources of that type. Use --dry-run=client first.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The --all flag deletes EVERY resource of the specified type in the namespace.

For example:
- kubectl delete pods --all: Kills all pods (services go down)
- kubectl delete svc --all: Removes all services (networking breaks)
- kubectl delete pvc --all: May delete all persistent data

Always preview first:
  kubectl delete <resource> --all --dry-run=client

Safer alternative:
  kubectl delete <resource> -l app=myapp  # Use label selectors

suggestions.description: Preview what would be deleted without making changes
Restart pods via deployment for graceful recreation
Delete a specific resource instead of all
Use label selectors for targeted deletion

structural_patterns.name: delete-all-namespaces
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl delete with -A/--all-namespaces affects ALL namespaces. Very dangerous!
structural_patterns.explanation:
The -A/--all-namespaces flag expands deletion to EVERY namespace in the cluster. This can take down your entire cluster:

- Production, staging, and dev environments affected
- System namespaces (kube-system) may be impacted
- Cross-namespace resources and dependencies break

This is almost never what you want. Always specify a namespace:
  kubectl delete <resource> -n <namespace>

Preview cluster-wide resources:
  kubectl get <resource> -A

structural_patterns.name: drain-node
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl drain evicts all pods from a node. Ensure proper pod disruption budgets.
structural_patterns.explanation:
kubectl drain evicts ALL pods from a node, typically for maintenance. This can cause service disruption:

- All pods are evicted (respecting PodDisruptionBudgets)
- DaemonSet pods remain unless --ignore-daemonsets is used
- Pods with local storage fail unless --delete-emptydir-data is used
- Without replicas elsewhere, services go down

Before draining:
  kubectl get pods -o wide | grep <node>  # Check what's running
  kubectl get pdb -A                       # Check disruption budgets

Safer approach:
  kubectl cordon <node>  # Prevent new pods first, then drain gradually

structural_patterns.name: cordon-node
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl cordon marks a node unschedulable. Existing pods continue running.
structural_patterns.explanation:
kubectl cordon marks a node as unschedulable. Existing pods continue running, but no new pods will be scheduled to this node.

Use cases:
- Preparing for maintenance
- Investigating node issues
- Gradual migration

To reverse:
  kubectl uncordon <node>

Check node status:
  kubectl get nodes
  kubectl describe node <node> | grep Taints

structural_patterns.name: taint-noexecute
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl taint with NoExecute evicts existing pods that don't tolerate the taint.
structural_patterns.explanation:
A NoExecute taint immediately evicts pods that don't have a matching toleration. This is more aggressive than NoSchedule:

- Existing pods are evicted (not just new scheduling blocked)
- Can cause immediate service disruption
- Pods may not have time for graceful shutdown

Check current taints:
  kubectl describe node <node> | grep Taints

Consider NoSchedule first:
  kubectl taint nodes <node> key=value:NoSchedule

Remove taint:
  kubectl taint nodes <node> key=value:NoExecute-

structural_patterns.name: delete-workload
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl delete deployment/statefulset/daemonset removes the workload. Use --dry-run first.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a workload terminates all its pods:

- Deployment: All replicas terminated, service goes down
- StatefulSet: Ordered shutdown, PVCs may be orphaned
- DaemonSet: Removed from all nodes
- ReplicaSet: Pods terminated (usually managed by Deployment)

Preview first:
  kubectl delete <type> <name> --dry-run=client
  kubectl get pods -l app=<name>  # Check affected pods

Consider scaling down first:
  kubectl scale deployment <name> --replicas=0

structural_patterns.name: delete-pvc
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl delete pvc may permanently delete data if ReclaimPolicy is Delete.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a PVC can cause permanent data loss depending on the PV's reclaimPolicy:

- Delete: Underlying storage is deleted (DATA LOST)
- Retain: PV is kept but becomes 'Released' (manual recovery needed)
- Recycle: Deprecated, data scrubbed

Check the reclaim policy:
  kubectl get pv <pv-name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy}'

Backup first:
  kubectl exec <pod> -- tar czf - /data > backup.tar.gz

Preview:
  kubectl delete pvc <name> --dry-run=client

suggestions.description: Check PVC status and usage before deleting
Find pods currently using this PVC
Preview deletion without making changes

suggestions.command: kubectl get pv $(kubectl get pvc {name} -o jsonpath='{.spec.volumeName}') -o jsonpath='{.spec.persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy}'
suggestions.description: Check reclaim policy to understand data fate

structural_patterns.name: delete-pv
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl delete pv may permanently delete the underlying storage.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a PersistentVolume can permanently destroy the underlying storage:

- Cloud disks (EBS, GCE PD, Azure Disk) may be deleted
- NFS mounts become orphaned
- Local storage data is lost

Even with Retain policy, deleting the PV may trigger storage cleanup.

Check what's using the PV:
  kubectl get pvc -A | grep <pv-name>

Check storage class policy:
  kubectl get storageclass <class> -o yaml

Preview:
  kubectl delete pv <name> --dry-run=client

structural_patterns.name: scale-to-zero
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl scale --replicas=0 stops all pods for the workload.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Scaling to zero replicas terminates ALL pods for the workload:

- Service becomes unavailable
- Endpoints are removed from Service
- In-flight requests are dropped
- StatefulSets: Ordered shutdown from highest ordinal

This is often intentional but can cause outages if done accidentally.

Check current replicas:
  kubectl get deployment <name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}'

To restore:
  kubectl scale deployment <name> --replicas=<N>

structural_patterns.name: delete-force
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl delete --force --grace-period=0 immediately removes resources without graceful shutdown.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Force deletion with zero grace period is dangerous:

- Pods are killed immediately (no SIGTERM, just gone)
- In-flight requests fail
- Data corruption risk if writes in progress
- Finalizers may be skipped (resource leak)

Kubernetes warns against this. Use only for stuck pods that won't terminate.

Try graceful deletion first:
  kubectl delete pod <name>                    # Default 30s grace
  kubectl delete pod <name> --grace-period=60  # Extended grace

Check why pod is stuck:
  kubectl describe pod <name> | grep -A5 Status

suggestions.description: Use default 30-second grace period for graceful shutdown
Extended grace period for slower shutdown
Check resource status to understand why it's stuck

structural_patterns.name: apply-force
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl apply --force deletes and recreates resources, causing downtime.
structural_patterns.explanation:
kubectl apply --force deletes the resource and recreates it from the manifest. This causes:

- Downtime as pods are terminated before new ones start
- Loss of any runtime modifications
- Potential data loss for stateful workloads
- Disruption to in-flight requests

Use this only when you cannot update resources normally due to immutable field changes.

Preview changes first:
  kubectl diff -f <file>

Try server-side apply for safer updates:
  kubectl apply --server-side -f <file>

suggestions.description: Apply without --force for in-place updates
Preview what changes would be applied
Use server-side apply for safer field management

structural_patterns.name: delete-from-directory
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl delete -f with directories or --recursive deletes many resources at once.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting from a directory or recursively removes ALL resources defined in those files:

- Multiple deployments, services, configmaps deleted at once
- Hard to recover if wrong directory
- No confirmation or preview by default

Always preview first:
  kubectl diff -f <directory>
  ls -la <directory>/*.yaml

Delete specific files instead:
  kubectl delete -f <specific-file.yaml>

suggestions.description: Delete from a specific file instead of directory
Preview what resources would be affected
Preview deletion without making changes

packs.name: Helm
packs.description: Protects against destructive Helm operations like uninstall and rollback without dry-run

structural_patterns.name: uninstall
structural_patterns.reason: helm uninstall removes the release and all its resources. Use --dry-run first.
structural_patterns.explanation:
helm uninstall deletes the release and ALL Kubernetes resources created by it:

- Deployments, services, and pods are terminated
- ConfigMaps and secrets are deleted
- Persistent volume claims may be deleted (depends on chart)
- Release history is purged (no rollback possible)

Safer alternatives:
- helm uninstall <release> --dry-run: Preview what will be deleted
- helm status <release>: Review current release state
- helm get all <release>: See all resources managed by release
- helm get manifest <release>: Get the actual Kubernetes manifests

structural_patterns.name: rollback
structural_patterns.reason: helm rollback reverts to a previous release. Use --dry-run to preview changes.
structural_patterns.explanation:
helm rollback reverts the release to a previous revision. This can cause unexpected behavior if the previous version differs significantly:

- Pod configurations are reverted (may break dependencies)
- ConfigMaps and secrets are rolled back
- Database migrations are NOT automatically undone
- Downtime may occur during the transition

Safer alternatives:
- helm rollback <release> <revision> --dry-run: Preview changes
- helm history <release>: Review available revisions
- helm diff rollback <release> <revision>: Compare changes (requires diff plugin)

structural_patterns.name: upgrade-force
structural_patterns.reason: helm upgrade --force deletes and recreates resources, causing downtime.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The --force flag causes Helm to delete and recreate resources instead of updating them in place. This can cause service disruption:

- Pods are terminated and recreated (downtime between)
- Persistent volume claims may be deleted and recreated
- In-flight requests are dropped during recreation
- Service IP addresses may change

Safer alternatives:
- Remove --force to use rolling updates
- helm upgrade --dry-run --debug: Preview changes
- helm diff upgrade: Compare before upgrading (requires diff plugin)

structural_patterns.name: upgrade-reset-values
structural_patterns.reason: helm upgrade --reset-values discards all previously set values.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The --reset-values flag discards all values from previous releases, using only chart defaults and explicitly provided values. This can unexpectedly change:

- Resource limits and replica counts
- Database connection strings and credentials
- Feature flags and environment variables
- Any customization from previous 'helm upgrade' commands

Safer alternatives:
- helm get values <release>: Review current values first
- helm upgrade --reuse-values: Keep existing values (default)
- helm upgrade -f values.yaml: Explicitly set all needed values

packs.name: Kustomize
packs.description: Protects against destructive Kustomize operations when combined with kubectl delete or applied without review

structural_patterns.name: kustomize-delete
structural_patterns.reason: kustomize build | kubectl delete removes all resources in the kustomization.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Piping kustomize build to kubectl delete removes ALL resources defined in the kustomization directory. This can delete entire applications:

- Every resource in kustomization.yaml and its bases is deleted
- Deployments, services, configmaps, secrets all removed
- Overlays may include resources you didn't expect
- No confirmation or preview by default

Safer alternatives:
- kustomize build <dir>: Review manifests first
- kustomize build <dir> | kubectl delete --dry-run=client -f -: Preview
- kustomize build <dir> | kubectl diff -f -: Compare with cluster state

structural_patterns.name: kubectl-kustomize-delete
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl kustomize | kubectl delete removes all resources in the kustomization.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Piping kubectl kustomize to kubectl delete removes ALL resources defined in the kustomization directory. This is equivalent to kustomize build | kubectl delete:

- Entire application stack can be deleted
- Base and overlay resources are all affected
- Includes resources from remote URLs if referenced
- Order of deletion may cause cascading failures

Safer alternatives:
- kubectl kustomize <dir>: Review manifests first
- kubectl delete --dry-run=client -k <dir>: Preview deletion
- kubectl diff -k <dir>: Compare with cluster state

structural_patterns.name: kubectl-delete-k
structural_patterns.reason: kubectl delete -k removes all resources defined in the kustomization. Use --dry-run first.
structural_patterns.explanation:
kubectl delete -k removes all resources defined in a kustomization directory. This is a convenient but dangerous shorthand:

- All resources in kustomization.yaml are deleted
- Includes base resources and all overlays
- May include namespaces, PVCs, and other critical resources
- No confirmation prompt by default

Safer alternatives:
- kubectl delete -k <dir> --dry-run=client: Preview what will be deleted
- kubectl kustomize <dir>: Review manifests before deleting
- kubectl get -k <dir>: List resources that would be affected
